Long ago, when stones remembered the rain before it fell, there was a river named Luma. 
She did not merely carry water; she carried songs from creatures who drank from her. 
Fishes sang lullabies in the eddies, birds chattered in the reeds, and children learned to skim logs. 
Yet Luma grew lonely, for the hills drank first from her throat and left nothing but memory. 
The mountains tasted her sweetness and stored it away, counting it like coins. 
Luma wished to share, yet every gift she gave vanished. 
One midsummer a traveler climbed the canyon and pressed a cracked cup to her shore. 
He asked for a drink, and she spilled herself so liberally that the cup filled and overflowed. 
The valley laughed with cool relief, and the traveler did not waste a drop but carried the surplus downstream. 
Where it fed a parched field and a tiny village. 
From that day, Luma learned to give, and the valley learned to receive. 
Rivers remember generosity as a rising tide. 
The river slept beneath the moon and whispered to the stars about balance. 
So every creature learned to share, and every season remembered to give back what it received. 
Harmony endured.
